claim_supported: Signaling games model how a sender's action (signal) conveys private information to a receiver who updates beliefs
key_result: Signals are costly and differentially costly for different types
limitation: enabling credible communication; receiver uses Bayesian updating to infer sender's type
extraction_confidence: Assumes rational players with common knowledge of rationality; may not capture bounded rationality

claim_supported: Bayesian persuasion involves a sender designing an information structure to influence receiver's actions via belief updating
key_result: Sender commits to a signaling scheme before state is realized; receiver updates beliefs using Bayes' rule and takes optimal action
limitation: Requires commitment to signaling scheme; may not be credible without commitment devices

query: Epistemic game theory and belief hierarchies
claim_supported: Epistemic game theory formalizes beliefs about beliefs (higher-order beliefs) and their role in strategic reasoning
key_result: Players' decisions depend on beliefs about others' beliefs; common knowledge of rationality can be modeled
limitation: Highly abstract; difficult to apply empirically without strong assumptions

claim_supported: Forward induction involves interpreting past actions as rational signals about future intentions
key_result: Players rationalize unexpected past actions as part of a larger rational strategy
extraction_confidence: Requires strong rationality assumptions; multiple forward-induction criteria exist

claim_supported: Game theory models of upstream/downstream signaling show how information flows influence beliefs and decisions
key_result: Downstream players can signal private information (e.g.
limitation: demand) to upstream players via actions like order quantities; upstream updates beliefs and adjusts production
extraction_confidence: Context-specific; assumes specific cost structures and information asymmetry
column12: Illustrates directional information flow analogous to epistemic retro-influence
